# Forensic Dossier — Etablissements Maurel & Prom S.A. (MAU.PA)

*Africa/SE-Asia/LatAm E&P · Euronext Paris · ~71% controlled by Pertamina (PIEP) · prepared as of 2026-06-21 from the 2025 Annual Results (12 Mar 2026), the Q1 2026 Activity release (15 Apr 2026), the 16/18 Feb 2026 Venezuela updates, and the screener financials. No FMP earnings-call transcript was bundled; the company holds activity calls but full Q&A transcripts are not public, so management "voice" is reconstructed from press releases and the results slideshow — quotes below are from those releases, not a verbatim call.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

Maurel & Prom's story over the last 18 months has migrated **from "diversified African gas-and-oil producer with a Seplat financial stake" to "post-disposal, net-cash, Venezuela-optionality oil producer under a Pertamina parent."** Three arcs run in parallel:

- **The Seplat exit (closed).** For years M&P carried a ~20% equity stake in Nigeria's Seplat Energy as a financial holding. In late 2025 it sold the entire stake for **$496m + a $10m top-up** ($248m received 31 Dec 2025, $248m early Feb 2026, $10m mid-Feb 2026). This is the dominant driver of the 2025 P&L — a **$287m disposal gain** — and converts a passive minority stake into hard cash on the balance sheet. The narrative shifted from "stake monetisation pending" to "monetised, now redeploy."
- **The Venezuela re-rating (the live story).** M&P Iberoamerica (an 80%-owned subsidiary) holds a 40% WI in the Urdaneta Oeste field. The licence was **suspended in May 2025** (US sanctions), crushing liftings. On **18 Feb 2026 OFAC issued General License 50A expressly naming M&P** as an authorised entity to conduct oil-and-gas operations in Venezuela with PDVSA. This is the single biggest narrative pivot of the period: from "stranded, suspended asset" to "re-authorised, resuming." Q1-26 Urdaneta production was still only **7,965 bopd (WI 40%)**, down 6% q/q on the suspension overhang, but a rig restarted in early March and management guides production up in Q2, "ahead of the launch of a drilling phase from 2027."
- **The growth-bolt-on that failed.** In 2025 M&P signed an SPA with BW Energy/Azule to buy into **Angola Block 14/14K**. By the Q1-26 release that **SPA had been TERMINATED and the $6m deposit fully reimbursed.** A growth leg the bulls had penciled in was quietly removed.

Net: the franchise has contracted toward its core producing assets (Gabon oil, Tanzania gas, Venezuela) while the balance sheet swelled with disposal cash. It is no longer a diversifying growth story; it is a **cash-rich, sanctions-optionality, parent-controlled cyclical.**

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The screener block must be read with care — several headline metrics are **artefacts of the Seplat gain and a stale price**, not run-rate reality.

| Management/scan claim | The hard number | Verdict |

The scan's net margin "70.9%" is a disposal artefact — flagged correctly in peer_comps as "anomalously high." |
| "Revenue $578m, down ~28% YoY" | Scan: rev 602M, -25.6% YoY | **Matches.** 2024 ($808m) was a high-oil year; 2025 fell on lower realised prices + the Venezuela suspension. The revenue decline is genuine, not optical. |

TTM FCF $110m is the cleaner run-rate. Either way FCF is **positive**, contradicting the scan's "P/FCF 0.0x" (a divide-by-stale-input). |

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Rising — but on commodity tailwind and a sanctions reprieve, not operational outperformance.** No verbatim Q&A is available; from the releases:

- **HY-2025 (Aug 2025):** defensive — the Venezuela licence suspension (May 2025) dominated, liftings frozen, tone cautious on the regulatory overhang.
- **2025 Annual (12 Mar 2026):** confident — "$428m net income," "$236m FCF," Seplat monetised, 15% dividend increase proposed (€0.33→€0.38). Offensive on capital return.
- **Q1-2026 (15 Apr 2026):** most confident of the period — GL 50A secured, oil at $90.8/b, sales +85% q/q, net cash $235m, rig restarted at Urdaneta. Forward-leaning ("drilling phase from 2027").

The shift from **defensive (sanctions) to offensive (re-authorised + high oil + dividend hike)** is real, but it is **contingent on two exogenous variables M&P does not control: the oil price and US sanctions policy.** Confidence is appropriate to the facts but not durable.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Medium.** M&P delivered on the things within its control: it closed the Seplat sale on the stated terms and timing, paid the 2025 dividend, raised the 2026 dividend 15%, and restarted the Urdaneta rig as promised. But the **Angola Block 14 SPA — explicitly signed and presented as a growth catalyst — was terminated** within ~6 months, which dings forward-commitment credibility. And the 2026 production framing (the early "42,700 boepd incl. 14,800 Gabon / 4,300 Angola / 10,000 Venezuela" figure that circulated) is **aspirational on Venezuela** — it assumes a lifting normalisation that is "in progress," not banked. Reliability on *banked* items: High. On *forward growth/Venezuela ramp*: Low-to-Medium.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE (cash-rich, parent-controlled cyclical) — with the catalyst already largely FIRED.** The single most important thing to weigh before sizing: **the apparent "248% margin of safety / P/E 4x" is a mirage built on a one-time $287m Seplat gain and a stale $8 price.** Strip the gain and the stock trades at roughly a *market* multiple of ~13-15x underlying earnings on a 9% ROIC African/Venezuelan E&P whose 2026 upside is (a) the oil price and (b) a sanctions reprieve that has **already been granted and partly priced** (stock doubled off its low). The €235m net cash is the only unambiguous, defensible value support.

**Economic moat: NONE/ERODING.** An E&P has no franchise moat — it sells an undifferentiated commodity at the spot price; the only durable edge is reserve quality and low-cost acreage, and here ROIC (9.1%) sits **below a fair country-risk-adjusted cost of capital.** Moat type: none (commodity price-taker); the "regulatory-license" angle (Venezuela GL 50A, host PSCs) is a *liability/reversibility*, not a moat. Returns are mediocre and not rising on an underlying basis once the gain is removed.

**Secular threat: MANAGEABLE (not terminal).** Long-run electrification/EV penetration caps the terminal value of pure fossil producers (the energy-transition-loser tail), but M&P's assets are **short-to-medium-cycle, low-cost, cash-returning African/LatAm oil & gas** — the demand peak is a multi-decade horizon, not an imminent impairment, and gas (Tanzania) has a longer runway. The nearer risk is **political/sanctions (Venezuela) and oil-price cyclicality**, which are recurring not secular. So: secular threat is real but **manageable**, theme = energy-transition-loser (carried, not acute).

The cheapness is **mostly a fear-print that has already partially reverted** (the sanctions overhang lifted, the stock doubled) layered on a **stale-input mirage** (the headline multiple). It is neither a deep-value bargain nor a structural value trap — it is a **fairly-valued, oil-beta, parent-controlled producer whose marquee catalyst (Venezuela re-authorisation) has already fired.**
